title: SQL Analysis & Reporting
subtitle: Turn one table into readable calculations, reliable KPIs, grouped scorecards, and decision-ready reports.
description: A cumulative SQLite reporting course covering readable output, null-safe calculations, aggregate functions, grouped summaries, conditional reporting, five module projects, and one final executive-report capstone.
audience: Learners who completed SQL Foundations / SQL V2.
Beginners who can already use SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT, LIKE, IN, BETWEEN, and NULL filters.
Learners preparing for practical reporting, analytics, and business-intelligence work.
prerequisites: Read a single database table and identify rows and columns.
Select specific columns and use readable filters.
Combine conditions with AND, OR, and NOT.
Sort and limit results and work with LIKE, IN, BETWEEN, and IS NULL.
goals: Create readable row-level reports with aliases, calculations, and appropriate rounding.
Handle missing values explicitly without disguising data-quality issues.
Build one-row KPI snapshots and grouped scorecards with well-defined grain.
Filter grouped results and calculate conditional performance measures.
Complete five cumulative module projects and one integrated executive report.

notes: Every technical lesson has two distinct sketches and two aligned code_input Try It exercises.
Every lesson quiz contains exactly two single-choice, two multi-choice, one reorder, and one fill-blank item.
Hints must explain the SQL idea or debugging path; never emit generic repair or test-taking language.
Examples, Try It tasks, quizzes, projects, and capstone steps must use distinct reporting questions and wording.
Each standard module has exactly one progressive module project; only the final topic is rendered as a capstone.
A progressive SQL step contains one cumulative replacement SELECT statement. The next starter equals the previous full solution.
Every threshold must be calibrated to sales_reporting seed data and return meaningful rows unless an empty result is the explicit teaching goal.
Do not request top-per-group, period-over-period growth, or any outcome requiring joins, subqueries, CTEs, or window functions.
All graded SQL must use sales_reporting and produce deterministic columns, values, and ordering when order matters.
